w:r.w:t: Field Specifications, Validation Rules & Developer Reference
Eight Tables Storing the CBV 2.0 Controlled Vocabulary

This document is the field-level technical specification for the eight database tables that store the Core Business Vocabulary used throughout the track-and-trace platform: cbv_bizstep, cbv_disposition, cbv_biz_txn_type, cbv_src_dest_type, cbv_error_reason, cbv_sensor_type, cbv_bizstep_disp_pair, and cbv_extension_namespace.

Where the CBV Vocabulary Reference document defines the values (what 'shipping' means, which dispositions exist, what bizSteps pair validly with which dispositions), and where the Database ERD shows the schema, this document specifies the fields of the tables that store those values — column definitions, frontend / backend / database validations, mandatory chips, regex patterns, sample valid and invalid values, cross-field rules, and developer notes.

This document follows the same field-card format as the GTIN, GLN, SGTIN, SSCC, and GIAI technical specifications. It is the implementation reference for the team building the CBV admin UI, the CBV REST API surface, and the ingestion-pipeline validator that checks every EPCIS event against these vocabularies.

The current platform design is a single-tenant application. CBV vocabularies are global — every value is shared across the entire installation, maintained by the platform admin role. There is no tenant_id column on any CBV table.

This is a simplification of the design recorded in the Database ERD document and the CBV Vocabulary Reference document, both of which describe a multi-tenant model with per-tenant CBV scoping. When multi-tenancy is added in a later phase, CBV tables will gain a tenant_id column with a composite primary key (tenant_id, code) and per-tenant seeding will be introduced.
All references to 'platform admin' in this document mean the single application-administrator role that manages the global CBV configuration.

The CBV vocabulary is the controlled enumeration of strings that may appear in standardised EPCIS event fields. Without a controlled vocabulary, every trading partner could invent their own values for the same concept (one says 'shipping', another says 'SHIPPED', a third says 'depart') and no two systems would be interoperable. The CBV is the GS1-curated agreement that defines exactly which strings are permitted in which fields.
1.1 Where the CBV tables fit in the data flow

The CBV tables sit between the EPCIS events table and the GS1 standard. They are the platform's local materialisation of the GS1 CBV 2.0 specification — the place where every standard value is recorded as a row, every value's URI is canonical, and every disabled value remembers it was once standard.
Master data tables (core_gtin_master, core_gln_master, core_sgtin_instance, etc.) describe the WHAT — products, locations, item instances, logistic units, assets.
Event tables (event, event_epc, event_biz_transaction, etc.) describe the WHEN and WHY — what happened to those identifiers and why.

CBV tables describe the LEGAL VALUES — the controlled vocabulary that the WHY can choose from. The bizStep on every event row is foreign-key-validated against cbv_bizstep.code. The disposition is validated against cbv_disposition.code. And so on.
1.2 Why eight tables and not one big lookup

CBV defines six distinct vocabularies for different field types (bizStep, disposition, business transaction type, source/destination type, error reason, sensor type). Each has its own URI namespace, its own enumeration of values, and its own platform-specific extension columns. Combining them into a single key-value table would require a 'vocab type' discriminator column and lose strict referential integrity at the database level.
Two additional tables support the six main vocabulary tables:

cbv_bizstep_disp_pair — the validity matrix for the bizStep × disposition combination. CBV 2.0 §6 specifies that certain pairings make sense (shipping + in_transit), others are doubtful (shipping + active), and a few are nonsensical (commissioning + destroyed). This table is the platform's authoritative enforcement of those rules.
cbv_extension_namespace — the registry of user-vocabulary namespace prefixes the platform allows. Without this registry, custom URIs in events would have no way to be distinguished from typos.
Every CBV row has a defined lifecycle:
How a row reaches this state
Platform admin adds a USER_VOCAB row but leaves enabled = FALSE
Not eligible to appear in events. Visible in admin UI for review.
Eligible to appear in incoming events. The ingestion validator accepts events that reference this value.
Row exists with enabled = FALSE; events referencing it are now quarantined
New events referencing this value are quarantined. Historical events that already reference this value still resolve correctly.
Re-enable → ACTIVE; or delete (USER_VOCAB rows only, if no events reference)
Hard DELETE — permitted only for USER_VOCAB rows with zero referencing events
Value no longer exists. Historical events are unaffected (their JSONB payload still has the URI verbatim) but the URI no longer resolves in admin UI lookups.

Section 2 lists the eight columns shared by the six main vocabulary tables (code, uri, definition, source, enabled, cbv_version, created_at — and the table-internal primary key code). Read this first.
Sections 3–8 cover the six main vocabulary tables in turn. For each: table purpose, table-specific extra columns (when any exist), and the DDL with indexes and expected row counts.
Section 9 covers cbv_bizstep_disp_pair — the pairing-matrix table.
Section 10 covers cbv_extension_namespace — the user-vocabulary namespace registry.
Section 11 defines the cross-field rules that span multiple CBV tables (referential integrity, source flag invariants, pair-matrix consistency).
Section 12 describes the seeding strategy and platform admin migration workflow.
Section 13 specifies the management API surface (CRUD endpoints, auth model).
Section 14 lists open questions and design decisions deferred to a future revision.
2. Common Columns — Shared by the Six Main Vocabulary Tables

The six main vocabulary tables (cbv_bizstep, cbv_disposition, cbv_biz_txn_type, cbv_src_dest_type, cbv_error_reason, cbv_sensor_type) share a common column structure. Define them once here; per-table sections only document the extras specific to that table.
The shape is: a code primary key, the canonical URI, a definition, a source flag (standard vs user vocab), an enabled flag, the CBV version it was seeded from, and a created-at timestamp.
Common Columns — Shared by the Six Main Vocabulary Tables
GS1 CBV 2.0 §3 — vocabulary value short name; final segment of the canonical CBV URI.
VARCHAR (column width varies per table — see per-table sections; range VARCHAR(40) to VARCHAR(80))
Lowercase ASCII, snake_case. Must match the final segment of the canonical CBV URI exactly.
M — primary key of the row; cannot be NULL.
Read-only on edit (renaming a code orphans every event that references it).
On create, validate against /^[a-z][a-z0-9_]{0,79}$/ — lowercase ASCII, must start with a letter, allow underscores and digits.
Maximum length depends on the table being edited; show the limit in the field hint.
Reject leading or trailing underscores, double underscores, uppercase, spaces, punctuation.
Reject reserved prefixes 'cbv_' and 'gs1_' on user-vocabulary entries (these are platform-reserved).
Show the resulting canonical URI live as the admin types: '{code}' → 'https://ref.gs1.org/cbv/{Vocab}-{code}'.
Regex enforcement: /^[a-z][a-z0-9_]{0,79}$/. Reject with 400 if violated.
Length check matches the column width of the target table.
Uniqueness check on (code) — duplicate insert returns 409 Conflict.
On UPDATE, the code column is immutable. Attempt to change it returns 422 Unprocessable Entity.
code VARCHAR(40|80) NOT NULL -- exact width per table
Note: no ON UPDATE CASCADE — codes are immutable once written.
— uppercase letter — must be all lowercase ASCII
— empty string — must be at least 1 character
Must be the last segment of the row's uri column. On insert, the backend canonicalises code → uri using the vocabulary's URI template; if both are supplied and mismatch, the request is rejected.

The code is the shortened display form used throughout the platform UI, in EPCIS event payloads (CBV-Compliant short-name form), and in foreign keys from the events store. Never display the full uri to operators except in developer/audit views. Codes are case-sensitive in storage but treated as case-insensitive at the API edge (the backend lowercases incoming code values before validation).
GS1 CBV 2.0 §3 — canonical absolute URI for the vocabulary value, dereferenceable on ref.gs1.org for GS1-standard values.
Absolute URI. For GS1 standard values: 'https://ref.gs1.org/cbv/{Vocab}-{code}'. For user-vocabulary values: must use a namespace prefix registered in cbv_extension_namespace.
M — every row must have a canonical URI.
Read-only on edit (URI is part of the public contract for the value).
On create with source = GS1_CBV_STANDARD, auto-generated from the URI template — do not show as an editable field.
On create with source = USER_VOCAB, allow custom input but validate the prefix is in the registered extension-namespace list.
Validate using a strict URI regex: must be 'https://' scheme, no spaces, no fragments.
URI parse must succeed; reject malformed URIs with 400.
Scheme must be 'https'. 'http' or other schemes are rejected.
For source = GS1_CBV_STANDARD: URI must match the canonical pattern for that vocabulary table (e.g., cbv_bizstep URIs must start with 'https://ref.gs1.org/cbv/BizStep-').
For source = USER_VOCAB: extract the namespace prefix and verify it exists in cbv_extension_namespace.namespace_uri. Reject 422 if not registered.
Uniqueness check on uri (independent of code) — two rows with the same URI are forbidden.
URL-encode the code segment if it contains URI-reserved characters (it shouldn't, given the code regex).
— namespace prefix 'my-co.example' not registered in cbv_extension_namespace
The final segment of the URI (after the rightmost '-') MUST equal the value of the code column. Backend rejects mismatches with 422.

When emitting EPCIS events in CBV-Compliant mode the platform writes the URI form. When emitting CBV-Compatible mode the platform writes the short-name code form. The two are interconvertible via this column. The URI is the long-term stable identifier — even if GS1 renames the short code in a future CBV release, the URI persists for historical events.
GS1 CBV 2.0 — natural-language definition of the value's meaning. Sourced verbatim from the CBV specification for GS1-standard rows.
Recommended ≤ 2000 characters; not enforced at DB level.
Plain English narrative. May include parenthetical clarifications. No formatting markup (no Markdown, no HTML).
C — optional for user-vocabulary additions, but strongly recommended. GS1-standard rows are always seeded with the published GS1 definition.
Multi-line textarea; soft limit 2000 characters with character counter.
Read-only for GS1_CBV_STANDARD rows (defined by GS1, not editable).
For USER_VOCAB rows: allow plain-text input with no markup.
Reject Markdown, HTML, or rich-text formatting on submit.
Strip leading/trailing whitespace; collapse internal runs of whitespace to single spaces.
On insert with source = GS1_CBV_STANDARD: definition is set by the platform seed migration; user-supplied values are ignored.
On insert with source = USER_VOCAB: accept any plain-text string up to a sensible upper bound (recommended 2000 chars).
Detect and reject HTML tags via regex: reject if definition matches /<[^>]+>/.
NULL or empty-string is acceptable for user-vocabulary rows.
definition TEXT -- no length constraint at DB; soft limit at the API
The process of transferring custody of items from one party to another, typically via a logistics provider.
The state of an item that has been deactivated through dispensing to a patient.
Internal quality check performed by the manufacturer before release to distribution.
— rejected because of internal multi-space runs (collapsed by BE)

The definition column is displayed in the admin UI's vocabulary browser, in tooltips next to dropdown choices, and in developer-facing API responses. Long definitions are truncated with an ellipsis in compact views. The platform never relies on the definition for validation logic — it is informational only.
Platform-internal column distinguishing rows seeded from the GS1 CBV standard vs. rows added by the platform admin as user vocabulary.
M — must be set on every row.
Not displayed as a user-editable field — set automatically by the create endpoint based on the context of the call.
Shown as a read-only badge in the row detail view: green badge 'GS1 Standard' or amber badge 'User Vocabulary'.
Filter on this column is exposed in the vocabulary browser (toggle: 'Show only user-added rows').
Set to 'GS1_CBV_STANDARD' by the seed migration. Never settable via the public API.
Set to 'USER_VOCAB' by the POST /v1/admin/cbv/{vocab} endpoint when a platform admin adds a new value.
Immutable after insert. UPDATE attempts return 422.
DELETE of a GS1_CBV_STANDARD row is blocked at the application layer (platform admin can disable via the enabled flag instead).
DELETE of a USER_VOCAB row is permitted only when no event in the event log references it.
Combined with the enabled flag determines whether a value is presented in dropdowns: a row is offered as a choice only when source IS NOT NULL AND enabled = TRUE.

The source flag is the basis of the CBV-version upgrade workflow. When GS1 publishes CBV 2.1, the migration runner filters strictly on source = 'GS1_CBV_STANDARD' to add new rows and mark deprecated ones — it never touches USER_VOCAB rows. Single-tenant note: in the current platform design there is no tenant scoping; the source flag is the only distinction between standard and admin-added values.
Platform-internal soft-disable flag. Lets the platform admin turn off a value without deleting the row (preserves audit trail).
Displayed as a toggle in the row detail view.
Toggling triggers a confirmation modal: 'Disabling will prevent future events from referencing this value. Existing event-log rows are unaffected.'
Disabled rows show a grey 'Disabled' badge in the vocabulary browser.
Disabled rows are excluded from the standard dropdown picker; an 'Include disabled' toggle on the picker lets admins still see them when investigating historical events.
Two-step undo: clicking Disable shows a 'Disabled — Undo' snackbar for 10 seconds.
On update via PATCH /v1/admin/cbv/{vocab}/{code} { enabled: false }: row is soft-disabled; no event-log rows are touched.
Re-enabling is permitted at any time and is idempotent.
Ingestion validator checks enabled = TRUE on every event field reference. Disabled values cause the event to be QUARANTINED, not rejected — the event is held in a quarantine queue for admin review.
Audit log entry written on every enabled toggle: actor, timestamp, before/after state, reason (if provided).
enabled BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE

Combined with source: a row is offered as a choice in the events-capture UI when enabled = TRUE; historical events that reference now-disabled values still resolve their URI and definition correctly for audit/replay purposes.

The enabled flag is the primary mechanism for retiring values without rewriting history. Hard-deletion of values is avoided wherever possible — disable, don't delete. The only case where DELETE is permitted is a freshly-added USER_VOCAB row that has never been used in any event.
Platform-internal column tracking which release of the GS1 CBV standard this row was seeded from.
Semantic version string: '2.0', '2.1', '1.2' etc. NULL for USER_VOCAB rows.
C — populated for GS1_CBV_STANDARD rows; NULL for USER_VOCAB rows.
Shown as a small subtitle under the value name in the vocabulary browser: 'CBV 2.0'.
Used as a filter facet: 'Show values added in CBV 2.0' / 'Show legacy CBV 1.2 values'.
Set by the seed migration to the migration's version constant.
Immutable post-insert except by a subsequent platform CBV-upgrade migration.
NULL is acceptable for USER_VOCAB rows.
-- NULL allowed; populated for GS1_CBV_STANDARD rows only
When the GS1 CBV version upgrade workflow runs, only rows with source = 'GS1_CBV_STANDARD' AND cbv_version < (target version) are eligible for migration. USER_VOCAB rows are always skipped.

Used by the seed-migration runner to determine whether a row is up-to-date with the platform's configured CBV version. The 'CBV release notes' panel in the admin UI queries this column to show what changed in each release.
Platform-internal audit timestamp — when this row was first inserted into the database.
Displayed in the row detail view as a localised timestamp with relative-time label: '12 April 2026 (2 weeks ago)'.
Not displayed in the compact vocabulary browser view.
Set by DEFAULT NOW() at insert time.
User-supplied values for this column are ignored.
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
All other CBV-related audit information (who toggled enabled, who added a user-vocab row) lives in the central audit-log table, not in the CBV row itself. This column tracks only the initial insert.

cbv_bizstep stores the controlled vocabulary of business steps — the verbs of supply-chain activity. bizStep answers 'what business process is happening?': commissioning, packing, shipping, receiving, inspecting, dispensing, destroying, and dozens more.

Every EPCIS event has a bizStep field. The ingestion validator looks up the event's bizStep value against cbv_bizstep.code; an unknown or disabled bizStep causes the event to be quarantined. The six main vocabulary tables all share this same lookup pattern.

GS1 CBV 2.0 defines approximately 41 bizStep values. The platform seeds all 41 at installation. Platform admin may add USER_VOCAB rows for industry-specific or company-internal business steps that CBV doesn't cover.
Initial seed: ~41 GS1_CBV_STANDARD rows. Steady state: 41 standard + 0–20 user vocabulary rows per installation.

Override of the common 'code' column: VARCHAR(80) — GS1-standard bizStep codes are short (commissioning, shipping, etc.) but user-vocabulary additions may be longer (internal_release_quality_check_v2)..
enabled BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE,
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
CREATE INDEX idx_cbv_bizstep_enabled ON cbv_bizstep (enabled) WHERE enabled = TRUE;
-- Used by the events-capture UI dropdown picker and the ingestion validator.

cbv_disposition stores the controlled vocabulary of dispositions — the states or conditions of an EPC at the time of an event. While bizStep is the verb (what is happening), disposition is the resulting state (what the EPC is now): in_transit, active, dispensed, destroyed, damaged, expired, recalled.

GS1 CBV 2.0 defines approximately 32 disposition values. The platform seeds all 32 at installation. Two additional CBV 2.0 concepts are encoded as table columns rather than separate vocabulary entries: typical_use distinguishes TRANSIENT (only-during-this-event) from PERSISTENT (carries forward as ongoing state) dispositions, and is_terminal flags dispositions that end the EPC's supply-chain lifecycle.
Initial seed: ~32 GS1_CBV_STANDARD rows. Steady state: 32 standard + 0–10 user vocabulary rows.
Override of the common 'code' column: VARCHAR(80) — for consistency with cbv_bizstep..
In addition to the common eight columns from Section 2, this table has the following extras:

CBV 2.0 §6 — Implementation Guideline distinguishes dispositions that are transient (apply only during one event window) from those that are persistent (carried forward as the item's continuing state until explicitly unset).
C — recommended but nullable; older GS1-seeded rows from CBV 1.2 may have NULL.
Single-select dropdown: 'Transient (only during this event)' or 'Persistent (carries forward as ongoing state)'.
Read-only for GS1_CBV_STANDARD rows seeded with CBV 2.0+.
Show inline help: 'Transient dispositions describe what happened in the event; persistent dispositions describe a state that continues until something else changes it (e.g. damaged, recalled).'
If the user picks PERSISTENT, also enable the is_terminal toggle (commonly co-occurring).
Reject any value outside the enum with 422.
Allow NULL on insert (some legacy rows lack this).
On UPDATE: changing TRANSIENT → PERSISTENT is permitted but logged as a notable audit event (this can change ingestion validation for new events).
CHECK (typical_use IS NULL OR typical_use IN ('TRANSIENT','PERSISTENT'))

Combined with is_terminal: PERSISTENT dispositions are eligible for use in persistentDisposition.set and persistentDisposition.unset arrays on EPCIS events (XCBV-007 — see Section 5 cross-field rules). TRANSIENT dispositions can only appear in the top-level disposition field.

Examples: 'in_transit' is TRANSIENT (only during shipping events). 'damaged', 'recalled', 'expired' are PERSISTENT (the pack stays damaged until something resets that). 'dispensed' and 'destroyed' are PERSISTENT and terminal (see is_terminal).
Platform-internal flag — TRUE when reaching this disposition is the end of the supply-chain lifecycle for the item. Combined with PERSISTENT it means the item is permanently out of circulation.
Displayed as a toggle in the disposition row detail view.
Disabled for editing on GS1_CBV_STANDARD rows.
Inline help: 'Terminal dispositions cannot be undone. Once an item reaches a terminal disposition, no further events should reference it as an active EPC.'
Show a red 'Terminal' badge next to terminal dispositions in dropdowns.

Ingestion validator: when an event references a terminal disposition on an EPC, that EPC's status is permanently frozen. Subsequent events on the same EPC (except for ERROR_DECLARATION corrections) are quarantined.
Cannot be toggled on a GS1_CBV_STANDARD row (the GS1 standard defines which values are terminal).
is_terminal BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE
If is_terminal = TRUE, typical_use must be PERSISTENT (XCBV-008). Terminal-but-transient is a nonsensical combination and is rejected at the application layer.

The ingestion pipeline maintains a denormalised 'latest_disposition' on the SGTIN / SSCC instance table. When latest_disposition.is_terminal = TRUE, the instance's lifecycle is frozen. Auditors can query terminal-state EPCs to confirm regulatory decommissioning (e.g. all packs dispensed in a pharmacy over a month).
enabled BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE,
CHECK (typical_use IS NULL OR typical_use IN ('TRANSIENT','PERSISTENT')),
is_terminal BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE,
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
CREATE INDEX idx_cbv_disposition_enabled ON cbv_disposition (enabled) WHERE enabled = TRUE;
CREATE INDEX idx_cbv_disposition_terminal ON cbv_disposition (is_terminal) WHERE is_terminal = TRUE;
-- The terminal index supports SGTIN/SSCC instance-status projection queries:
-- 'is this disposition one that freezes the EPC's lifecycle?'

cbv_biz_txn_type stores the controlled vocabulary of business transaction types — the kinds of business documents that an EPCIS event may reference. Examples: po (purchase order), inv (invoice), desadv (despatch advice), bol (bill of lading), recadv (receiving advice).

GS1 CBV 2.0 defines approximately 13 business transaction types. The platform seeds all 13 at installation. The bizTransactionList on EPCIS events typically references these types to link a custody-transfer event back to its source business document.
Note: TransactionEvent (one of the five EPCIS event types) requires at least one bizTransaction. The validator enforces this via rule CBV-009 documented in the CBV Vocabulary Reference.
Initial seed: ~13 GS1_CBV_STANDARD rows. Steady state: 13 standard + 0–5 user vocabulary rows.
Override of the common 'code' column: VARCHAR(40) — biz_txn_type codes are short (po, inv, desadv) so a tighter column width is appropriate..
enabled BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE,
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
CREATE INDEX idx_cbv_biz_txn_type_enabled ON cbv_biz_txn_type (enabled) WHERE enabled = TRUE;

cbv_src_dest_type stores the controlled vocabulary of source/destination types used in the sourceList and destinationList of EPCIS events. Three values: owning_party (who owns the goods), possessing_party (who physically holds them), and location (where they are).

Each type fixes which kind of URI must appear with it: owning_party and possessing_party must reference PGLNs (party GLNs); location must reference SGLNs (location GLNs). This is encoded in the required_uri_type column and enforced at event ingestion.
Initial seed: 3 GS1_CBV_STANDARD rows. Steady state: 3 standard + rare user vocabulary additions (this vocabulary is unusually closed).
Override of the common 'code' column: VARCHAR(40)..
In addition to the common eight columns from Section 2, this table has the following extras:
CBV 2.0 §5 — defines for each Source/Destination type whether the source/destination URI must be a PGLN (party — owning_party, possessing_party) or an SGLN (location).
M — set on every row; needed by the ingestion validator.
Single-select dropdown: 'PGLN — Party GLN (https://id.gs1.org/417/...)' or 'SGLN — Location SGLN (https://id.gs1.org/414/...)'.
Read-only for GS1_CBV_STANDARD rows (defined by GS1).
Show inline help with the regex of the expected URI format alongside the choice.
Reject any value outside the enum with 422.
Used by the ingestion validator: for each source/destination on an incoming event, look up the type's required_uri_type and validate the corresponding URI matches it.
— not in enum — each type fixes one URI shape
Drives XCBV-009: event source/destination URI shape must match required_uri_type of the source/destination type. owning_party + SGLN URI = QUARANTINE. location + PGLN URI = QUARANTINE.

GS1 CBV 2.0 defines three source/destination types: owning_party (→ PGLN), possessing_party (→ PGLN), and location (→ SGLN). These are seeded into cbv_src_dest_type at platform install and are the canonical reference for the ingestion validator's URI-shape check.
enabled BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE,
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
CREATE INDEX idx_cbv_src_dest_type_enabled ON cbv_src_dest_type (enabled) WHERE enabled = TRUE;

cbv_error_reason stores the controlled vocabulary of error reasons used in EPCIS event errorDeclaration blocks. When a previously-submitted event is corrected (e.g. wrong EPC scanned, incorrect timestamp), the platform emits a correcting event with an errorDeclaration referencing one of these reasons.

GS1 CBV 2.0 defines approximately 3 error reasons: incorrect_data, did_not_occur, and other. Despite the small size, this is one of the most operationally important vocabularies — every audit-trail correction touches it.
Initial seed: 3 GS1_CBV_STANDARD rows. Steady state: 3 standard + rare user vocabulary additions.
Override of the common 'code' column: VARCHAR(40)..
enabled BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE,
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
CREATE INDEX idx_cbv_error_reason_enabled ON cbv_error_reason (enabled) WHERE enabled = TRUE;

cbv_sensor_type stores the controlled vocabulary of sensor measurement types — the kinds of physical measurements a sensor can produce. Examples: Temperature, Humidity, Tilt, Shock, AbsoluteHumidity, ConductivityMeasurement.

GS1 CBV 2.0 defines approximately 10 pharma-relevant sensor types (CBV 2.0 broadened the list significantly for food-safety applications, but the platform seeds only the pharma-relevant subset). Each type has a default unit of measure expressed as a UN/CEFACT Recommendation 20 code (CEL = degrees Celsius, P1 = percent, etc.).

Sensor events (EPCIS 2.0 sensorElementList) reference these types in the sensorReport.type field. The ingestion validator checks that every sensorReport's type is a known cbv_sensor_type and that the uom is a valid UN/CEFACT Rec 20 code.
Initial seed: ~10 GS1_CBV_STANDARD rows (pharma-relevant subset). Steady state: 10 standard + 0–5 user vocabulary rows.
Override of the common 'code' column: VARCHAR(40) — sensor types use CamelCase in GS1's URI scheme (Temperature, Humidity), so the code column stores the lowercase canonical form..
In addition to the common eight columns from Section 2, this table has the following extras:
GS1 CBV 2.0 §7 — sensor measurement types. UN/CEFACT Recommendation 20 / ECE/TRADE/424 unit-of-measure codes (e.g. CEL = degrees Celsius, KEL = kelvin, P1 = percent, GRM = gram).
Up to 10 characters (UN/CEFACT codes are typically 2-3 characters)
UN/CEFACT Rec 20 alphanumeric code, uppercase. NULL permitted if the sensor type has no obvious default.
C — recommended, NULL allowed for unusual sensor types.
Autocomplete dropdown sourced from a static reference list of UN/CEFACT Rec 20 codes.
Show both the code and the human label: 'CEL — Degrees Celsius'.
Inline help: 'The default unit shown when an operator records a reading of this sensor type. Operators can still override per-reading.'
Validate against the embedded UN/CEFACT Rec 20 code list (a static JSON file shipped with the platform).
— must be UN/CEFACT code, not text
— must be UN/CEFACT code, not symbol

Sensor reports on EPCIS events specify their own uom. The ingestion validator compares the event's uom with the type's default_uom and emits an INFO log if they disagree (legitimate but worth flagging).

The UN/CEFACT Rec 20 code list is large (>1500 codes) but the pharma-relevant subset is small (~30 codes). The platform ships a curated subset in the admin-UI autocomplete; the full list is available via a secondary lookup. The 'common pharma sensor types' are: Temperature (CEL), Humidity (P1), Tilt (DD — degree), Shock (MSK — m/s², or NEW for newton).
enabled BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE,
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
CREATE INDEX idx_cbv_sensor_type_enabled ON cbv_sensor_type (enabled) WHERE enabled = TRUE;

cbv_bizstep_disp_pair is the validity matrix for combinations of bizStep and disposition. CBV 2.0 §6 Implementation Guideline specifies that certain bizStep/disposition pairings are canonical (shipping + in_transit), some are unusual but legal (shipping + damaged when goods become damaged in transit), and a few are nonsensical (commissioning + destroyed).

This table is the platform's authoritative enforcement of those rules. Every event that has both a bizStep and a disposition is checked against this table at ingestion time. If the pair is VALID, the event proceeds silently. If WARN, the event is accepted with a logged warning. If INVALID, the event is quarantined for manual review. Unknown combinations (no row in this table) are treated as implicit WARN.

Seeded: ~20 explicit VALID rows for the most-common pharma combinations and ~5 explicit INVALID rows for nonsensical pairings. Most of the 41×32 = ~1300 possible combinations are deliberately left absent — those fall through to the implicit WARN default. Total expected rows: ~25–50 over the lifetime of the platform.
Unlike the six main vocabulary tables, the pair table has its own shape — no shared common columns. Each column is specified here:
GS1 CBV 2.0 §6 Implementation Guideline — the bizStep value being paired with a disposition.
Foreign key reference — must equal an existing cbv_bizstep.code (no implicit creation).
Source: dropdown sourced from cbv_bizstep WHERE enabled = TRUE.
On selection, show the disposition picker pre-filtered to dispositions that don't already have a pair with this bizstep (avoid duplicates).
If the admin tries to add a pairing that already exists, show 'A rule already exists for this combination — open the existing one to edit'.
FK enforcement: bizstep_code MUST exist in cbv_bizstep. Reject 422 if not.
Composite PK uniqueness check: (bizstep_code, disposition_code) must be unique.
Immutable on UPDATE — to change the pairing's bizstep, delete and re-create.
Pair (bizstep_code, disposition_code) must be unique. Both columns are FKs; deleting from cbv_bizstep or cbv_disposition is blocked by the FK while a pairing exists.

The pairing matrix is the central enforcement point for the CBV-003 / CBV-004 / CBV-005 / CBV-006 ingestion rules. When a new bizStep is added via user vocabulary, the admin should also add the appropriate pairings or the validator falls back to 'WARN' for unknown combinations.
GS1 CBV 2.0 §6 — the disposition value being paired with the bizStep.
Foreign key reference — must equal an existing cbv_disposition.code.
Source: dropdown sourced from cbv_disposition WHERE enabled = TRUE.
Pre-filter the dropdown to dispositions not yet paired with the selected bizstep.
If the admin selects a terminal disposition (is_terminal = TRUE), show a contextual hint: 'Terminal dispositions are typically paired only with terminal-action bizSteps (dispensing, destroying).'
FK enforcement: disposition_code MUST exist in cbv_disposition. Reject 422 if not.
Platform-internal classification of the bizStep × disposition pairing into one of three ingestion-validation outcomes.
Three-way segmented control labelled 'When this combination appears in an event…':
• Amber WARN — 'Accept but log a warning for review.'
• Red INVALID — 'Quarantine the event for manual review.'
Show example contexts in inline help: 'shipping + in_transit → VALID. shipping + dispensed → INVALID — dispensing is a terminal disposition that cannot occur during shipping.'
Default selection on a new pair: VALID. The admin must consciously choose WARN or INVALID.
Reject anything outside the enum with 422.
Used by the ingestion validator to dispatch incoming events. The rule is:
• WARN → log a structured warning entry but accept the event.
• INVALID → quarantine the event for admin review.
Toggling validity from VALID to INVALID does NOT retroactively quarantine historical events — only events arriving after the change are affected.

Combinations not present in the pair table are treated by the ingestion validator as implicitly WARN (unknown pairing, accept with warning). To make an unknown pairing strictly invalid, an explicit INVALID row must be inserted.

Recommended seeding: only the 20 or so most-common pharma pairings get explicit VALID rows; the few known-invalid combinations (shipping+dispensed, commissioning+destroyed) get explicit INVALID rows; everything else falls through to the implicit-WARN default. This keeps the table compact and editable. The CBV-005 / CBV-006 / CBV-008 ingestion rules use this column at runtime.
Platform-internal column. Free-text rationale explaining why this pairing is VALID / WARN / INVALID. Surfaced to admins reviewing quarantined events.
Inline help: 'A short explanation visible in event-quarantine review screens. Leave blank if obvious.'
Pre-fill with common templates when validity = WARN or INVALID: 'WARN — unusual pairing seen in supply-chain edge cases.' / 'INVALID — terminal disposition cannot occur at this lifecycle step.'
No format validation beyond plain-text safety (reject HTML tags).
Shipping + in_transit is the canonical pairing for distribution-phase movement.
Commissioning + destroyed makes no sense — commissioning is creation, destroying is termination.
The note column is shown alongside the validity badge in the event-quarantine review UI, so investigators reviewing a flagged event know why it was flagged. Keep notes short and concrete.
-- The pair PK already covers (bizstep_code, disposition_code) which is the
-- primary lookup at ingestion time. The two single-column indexes support
-- 'show me all pairings involving disposition X' admin queries.
INSERT INTO cbv_bizstep_disp_pair (bizstep_code, disposition_code, validity, note) VALUES
('commissioning', 'active', 'VALID', 'Creating a new SGTIN/SSCC sets it active.'),
INSERT INTO cbv_bizstep_disp_pair (bizstep_code, disposition_code, validity, note) VALUES
INSERT INTO cbv_bizstep_disp_pair (bizstep_code, disposition_code, validity, note) VALUES
('shipping', 'damaged', 'WARN', 'Damage discovered in transit — legal but flag.'),
('receiving', 'damaged', 'WARN', 'Damage at receipt — legal but flag for QC.');

cbv_extension_namespace registers the URI prefixes the platform allows as namespaces for user-vocabulary additions. When the platform admin adds a USER_VOCAB row in any of the six main vocabulary tables, the row's uri column must reference a namespace prefix that exists in this registry.

Without this registry, USER_VOCAB additions could use any URI prefix, which would prevent the ingestion validator from distinguishing 'legitimate user vocabulary' from 'typo or malicious value'. The registry is the explicit allow-list.
0 rows at installation (the platform doesn't seed any user namespaces). One or two rows per company that wants to add their own vocabulary terms. Total expected: 0–5 rows.
Platform-internal. The short prefix used in JSON-LD @context to denote a user-vocabulary URI namespace (e.g. 'mycopharma' in 'mycopharma:internal_qc_check').
Lowercase ASCII, alphanumeric, may include digits but not underscores or hyphens.
Regex /^[a-z][a-z0-9]{0,39}$/ — lowercase ASCII, must start with a letter, no separators.
Read-only on edit (renaming a prefix breaks every URI that references it).
Reject reserved prefix list at API edge.
Uniqueness check on (prefix) — duplicate insert returns 409.

Every USER_VOCAB row in the six main vocabulary tables MUST have its URI's namespace prefix present in this registry. The backend validates uri against cbv_extension_namespace.namespace_uri at insert time.

The prefix is also what appears in the EPCIS event's @context to map the short namespace to the full URI. For 'mycopharma:internal_qc_check' to be valid in an EPCIS event, the event must declare '@context: { "mycopharma": "https://my-co.example/cbv-ext/" }' (or rely on the platform's auto-injected context).
Platform-internal. The full URI prefix that the short namespace prefix maps to.
Absolute URI, https scheme, ending in '/' or '#'.
URI validation: https scheme, well-formed, ends with '/' or '#'.

Show inline help: 'This is the URI prefix. A USER_VOCAB row with prefix "mycopharma" and namespace URI "https://my-co.example/cbv-ext/" lets you use URIs like https://my-co.example/cbv-ext/internal_qc_check.'
Reject if it doesn't end with '/' or '#'.
Reject 'https://ref.gs1.org/' as a USER namespace (that's reserved for GS1 standard rows).
Used by the URI validation logic on the six main vocabulary tables: a USER_VOCAB row's uri column must start with one of the registered namespace_uri values.

The namespace URI is the part that gets injected into EPCIS event @context when the platform emits events using these user-vocabulary values. The platform's egress pipeline auto-builds the @context from all registered cbv_extension_namespace rows used in the event.
Platform-internal. Optional JSON-Schema describing the expected shape of values in this namespace. Used for advanced validation when user vocabulary carries structured payloads.
Inline help: 'Advanced — JSON-Schema describing the shape of values in this namespace. Most user vocabularies are simple URIs and don't need a schema.'
Leave blank for the common case.
If present, validate against the JSON-Schema meta-schema.
NULL is acceptable (most rows will have this NULL).
-- No CHECK constraint; structural validation happens at the application layer.
— not valid JSON-Schema (type must be a string or array)
When set, the ingestion validator uses this schema to validate the values in the corresponding USER_VOCAB rows' uri column suffix (rare; advanced use).

Reserved for advanced use cases (e.g. when a user-vocabulary value is parameterised: 'mycopharma:internal_qc_check?revision=3'). Most pharma user-vocabulary entries are plain enumerated values and need no schema.
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
No additional indexes beyond the primary key and UNIQUE constraint. The table is small enough that full-table scans are cheap.
-- A platform admin adds the company's own namespace for internal vocabulary
-- Now this URI is allowed in USER_VOCAB rows:
INSERT INTO cbv_bizstep (code, uri, definition, source, enabled) VALUES
'Internal quality check performed by manufacturer before release.',
-- Events can now legally reference this vocabulary value.

Rules that span multiple columns or multiple CBV tables. These are the constraints the application, database, and ingestion validator enforce together to keep the vocabulary tables internally consistent and the event log audit-safe.
Every row must have exactly one source value. source = 'GS1_CBV_STANDARD' identifies a row seeded from the GS1 standard. source = 'USER_VOCAB' identifies a row added by the platform admin.
DB-level CHECK + application-level immutability on UPDATE.
URI uniqueness — no two rows in the same table may share the same uri column value.
code must equal the final segment of uri (after the rightmost '-'). E.g. uri = 'https://ref.gs1.org/cbv/BizStep-shipping' requires code = 'shipping'.
Application-layer validation on INSERT; rejected with 422 if mismatched.
All six main vocabulary tables + cbv_extension_namespace
USER_VOCAB rows must have a uri whose prefix matches a namespace_uri registered in cbv_extension_namespace.
Application-layer validation on INSERT; rejected with 422 if namespace not registered.
GS1_CBV_STANDARD rows are immutable except for the enabled flag. The code, uri, definition, source, and cbv_version columns cannot be UPDATEd via the API on a GS1_CBV_STANDARD row.
GS1_CBV_STANDARD rows cannot be DELETEd. Use enabled = FALSE to retire them.

Persistent dispositions (typical_use = 'PERSISTENT') are eligible for use in EPCIS event persistentDisposition.set and persistentDisposition.unset arrays. Transient dispositions (TRANSIENT) cannot appear there.
Ingestion validator at event capture (rule CBV-012).
Terminal dispositions (is_terminal = TRUE) must be persistent (typical_use = 'PERSISTENT'). Terminal-but-transient is nonsensical and forbidden.

Each src_dest_type row defines required_uri_type. Event source/destination URIs must match: PGLN types require URIs of the form https://id.gs1.org/417/... (party GLN). SGLN types require https://id.gs1.org/414/... (location SGLN).
Ingestion validator at event capture (rules CBV-007 and CBV-008).
Both bizstep_code and disposition_code in a pair must reference existing rows (any source, any enabled state) in cbv_bizstep and cbv_disposition respectively.
validity must be one of VALID, WARN, INVALID. Unknown pairings (no row) are treated as implicit WARN at runtime.

These are SOFT FOREIGN KEYS — the ingestion validator checks the value exists and is enabled = TRUE at event-capture time, but there is no hard DB-level FK. This preserves the audit trail when a value is later disabled.
Ingestion validator (rule CBV-002). Documented in the EPCIS Events specification.
namespace_uri must end with '/' or '#'. The 'https://ref.gs1.org/' prefix is reserved for GS1_CBV_STANDARD rows and cannot be registered as a user namespace.
DELETE of a USER_VOCAB row is permitted only when no event in the event log references it. The platform performs a guard SELECT before allowing DELETE.
Application-layer guard query against the event store.

When the platform's configured CBV version changes (e.g. from 2.0 to 2.1), the migration runner adds new rows with source = 'GS1_CBV_STANDARD' and cbv_version = '2.1', marks deprecated rows as enabled = FALSE, and NEVER touches USER_VOCAB rows.
Migration runner logic; documented in Section 12.

At platform installation, a single migration script (V001__cbv_seed.sql) inserts all GS1 CBV 2.0 standard values into the six main vocabulary tables. The eight cross-table pair rules and the URI namespace registry remain empty — admins populate those manually after install.
Total rows seeded: ~41 bizSteps + ~32 dispositions + ~13 biz_txn_types + 3 src_dest_types + 3 error_reasons + ~10 sensor_types ≈ 102 rows.
Every seeded row has source = 'GS1_CBV_STANDARD', enabled = TRUE, cbv_version = '2.0'.
The cbv_bizstep_disp_pair table is populated by a separate seed file (V002__cbv_pair_seed.sql) with the ~25 canonical pairings shown in Section 9.5.
The cbv_extension_namespace table is NOT seeded — it remains empty until the platform admin adds the first user-vocabulary namespace.
INSERT INTO cbv_bizstep (code, uri, definition, source, cbv_version) VALUES
'Creation and identification of new SGTIN/SSCC/GIAI instances.',
'Transferring goods from one party''s custody to another via a logistics provider.',
'Taking custody of goods from a shipment.',
'Pharmacy dispense of a unit to a patient — typically terminal.',
-- disposition entries (extract — with typical_use and is_terminal)
INSERT INTO cbv_disposition (code, uri, definition, source, cbv_version, typical_use, is_terminal) VALUES
'The item is in its normal active state in the supply chain.',
'The item is currently moving between custodians.',
'The item has been dispensed to a patient — terminal.',
INSERT INTO cbv_src_dest_type (code, uri, definition, required_uri_type, source, cbv_version) VALUES
'The party that owns the goods at the time of the event.',
'The party that physically holds the goods at the time of the event.',
'The physical location where the event takes place.',
When GS1 publishes a new CBV version (e.g. 2.1), the platform admin upgrades the configuration. The upgrade is performed by an automatic migration script that runs at the next deployment:
Step 1 — Add new rows. INSERT all values introduced in the new CBV version. New rows have source = 'GS1_CBV_STANDARD', enabled = TRUE, cbv_version = '2.1'.

Step 2 — Mark deprecated rows. For values that GS1 marked as DEPRECATED in the new version (e.g. 'no_pedigree_match' deprecated in CBV 2.0), the migration sets enabled = FALSE. The row is preserved for historical events but no longer offered in new event capture.

Step 3 — Update existing definitions. If GS1 revised a value's definition without renaming, the migration UPDATEs the definition column on the existing row. The uri and code columns are never changed (would orphan historical events).
Step 4 — Never touch USER_VOCAB rows. The migration filters strictly on source = 'GS1_CBV_STANDARD'. Platform-admin-added user vocabulary is preserved untouched across the upgrade.
Step 5 — Update platform CBV version constant. The platform's application-config 'configured CBV version' value is updated to reflect the new release.
-- V050__cbv_upgrade_2_1.sql — Example upgrade from CBV 2.0 to 2.1
-- 1) Add new bizStep values introduced in 2.1
INSERT INTO cbv_bizstep (code, uri, definition, source, cbv_version) VALUES
-- 3) Update definitions for renamed concepts (URI unchanged)
-- 4) Update platform CBV-version stamp on rows that are now 2.1-compliant

CRUD endpoints for the eight CBV tables, scoped to the platform-admin role. These endpoints are mounted under /v1/admin/cbv and require an authenticated admin session. They are NOT exposed to tenant API clients (in the future multi-tenant model, this changes; in the current single-tenant model, all CBV management is platform-admin only).
List bizStep vocabulary entries. Query params: enabled, source, cbv_version, cursor.
Add a USER_VOCAB bizStep row. Server sets source = 'USER_VOCAB', enabled = TRUE, cbv_version = NULL.
Toggle enabled, or update definition (USER_VOCAB rows only). Reason field required for audit log.
Delete a USER_VOCAB row. Returns 409 if referenced by events.
Same set of five endpoints for the other five vocabulary tables
/v1/admin/cbv/{vocab} where vocab ∈ { bizstep, disposition, biz_txn_type, src_dest_type, error_reason, sensor_type }.
List bizstep × disposition pairings. Query params: bizstep_code, disposition_code, validity, cursor.
Add a pairing. Required body fields: bizstep_code, disposition_code, validity, optionally note.
Update validity or note on an existing pairing.
Remove a pairing — falls back to implicit-WARN at ingestion.
Register a new namespace prefix + URI. Required: prefix, namespace_uri. Optional: schema_json.
Remove a namespace registration. Returns 409 if USER_VOCAB rows reference it.
All list endpoints return paginated results with the same envelope used elsewhere on the platform (cursor-based, see EPCIS REST API specification).
// → 201 Created with the persisted row (source set to USER_VOCAB by server)
"reason": "Deprecating local internal_qc_check in favour of inspecting."
// → 204 No Content (USER_VOCAB rows with no event refs)
// → 422 Unprocessable Entity (GS1_CBV_STANDARD row — use PATCH enabled=false instead)
All /v1/admin/cbv endpoints require an authenticated session with role = PLATFORM_ADMIN.
API key bearer auth (HTTP Authorization: Bearer ...) is the only supported method for these endpoints.
Every state-changing request (POST, PATCH, DELETE) writes an audit log entry with the actor's identity, the action, and the before/after state.
Rate limits apply: 100 requests/minute per admin session — these endpoints are low-volume.
Malformed JSON, missing required fields, wrong content type.
Returned for any non-admin session hitting /v1/admin/cbv.
Vocabulary code or pair does not exist
Returned by GET/PATCH/DELETE on nonexistent rows.
Duplicate code on POST, or DELETE blocked by event-log refs
code regex fail, uri/code mismatch, immutable column UPDATE attempt, GS1_CBV_STANDARD DELETE.
Design decisions and edge cases parked for a future revision. Most are low-priority; a few have downstream implications worth deciding before significant CBV-related code is shipped.

The current design is single-tenant. When multi-tenancy is reintroduced (per the deferred tenant_id design captured in the Database ERD doc), the CBV tables will gain a tenant_id column with composite PK (tenant_id, code) and per-tenant seeding at tenant creation. Confirm: does each tenant get an independent copy, or do tenants share a global standard set with per-tenant overrides? Recommended: each tenant gets an independent copy seeded at tenant creation (simpler reasoning, slightly more storage).

When the platform emits events (rather than ingests them), should the output default to CBV-Compliant short-name form (code only, no custom URIs) or CBV-Compatible form (full URIs that may include user vocabulary)? Recommended default: CBV-Compliant. Per-partner override on the subscription/named_query level is the more flexible path; revisit when subscriptions are productised.

CBV 2.0 deprecated 'no_pedigree_match' but the value remains valid in the JSON-LD context. Should events that reference deprecated values be WARNed, ERRORed, or silently accepted? Recommended: WARN on first occurrence per CBV-version cycle, then silently accept (avoid alert fatigue).

Can the same short code (e.g. 'qc_check') appear as a USER_VOCAB row in multiple vocabulary tables (cbv_bizstep AND cbv_disposition)? Currently the answer is yes — each table has independent PKs. Confirm this is intentional. The risk is operator confusion if the same word means two different things in two contexts. Recommended: allow, but the admin UI shows a warning when adding a code that already exists in another vocabulary.

The matrix is sparse by design — explicit VALID rows for canonical pairings, explicit INVALID for known-nonsense pairings, implicit WARN for everything else. Confirm this is the right default for pharma. Some regulators (Tatmeen in particular) may prefer strict enforcement: 'every event must reference a pair that is explicitly VALID; unknown pairings are quarantined.' This would invert the default — and would require seeding hundreds of explicit VALID rows. Decision deferred until L5 UAE extension specification is built.

CBV definitions are English only (GS1 source). For the UAE market, Arabic translations of vocabulary definitions may be expected by some pharma operators. Confirm whether definition is stored only in English or whether the platform adds a definition_translations JSONB column. Recommended for current scope: English only; defer translations to a later product release.

The doc references an 'audit log' for tracking enabled toggles and other state changes. The audit_log table itself is not specified here — it is a cross-cutting concern shared by all admin endpoints. Decision: audit_log table specification belongs in its own document (or in the RBAC/security spec). Confirm and add to backlog.

CBV 2.0 defines a broad sensor-type vocabulary including food-safety-specific values (microorganism concentrations, chemical residue measurements). The platform should seed only the pharma-relevant subset (Temperature, Humidity, Tilt, Shock, ~10 types). Confirm which CBV sensor types qualify as pharma-relevant. Recommended list documented in the CBV Vocabulary Reference document.
